questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
If $(1+x)^n=C_0+C_1 x+C_2 x^2+\ldots \ldots+C_n x^n$, then the sum of the product of the coefficients taken two at a time can be represented by $\sum_{i=0}^n \sum_{j=i+1}^n C_i C_j=2^a-\frac{b !}{c(d !)^2}$.
Then which of the following are correct?
questions.solution:
Note that $C_k$ is binomial coefficient ${ }^n C_k$.
$\left(\sum_{i=0}^n C_i\right)^2=\sum_{i=0}^n C_i^2+2 \sum_{i=0}^n \sum_{j=i+1}^n C_i C_j$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=0}^n \sum_{j=i+1}^n C_i C_j=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\sum_{i=0}^n C_i\right)^2-\sum_{i=0}^n C_i^2\right]$
$=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\sum_{i=0}^n{ }^n C_i\right)^2-\sum_{i=0}^n\left({ }^n C_i\right)^2\right]=\frac{1}{2}\left[2^{2 n}-{ }^{2 n} C_n\right]=2^{2 n-1}-\frac{(2 n) !}{2(n !)^2}$
Therefore, $a=2 n-1, b=2 n, c=2, d=n$ all are true.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Let $\left(1+x^2\right)^2(1+x)^n=A_0+A_1 x+A_2 x^2+\ldots \ldots$. If $A_0, A_1, A_2$ are in A.P. then the value of $n$ is
questions.solution: $\left(1+x^2\right)^2(1+x)^n=\left(1+2 x^2+x^4\right)\left({ }^n C_0+{ }^n C_1 x+{ }^n C_2 x^2+\ldots \ldots\right)$ $={ }^n \mathrm{C}_0+{ }^n \mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{x}+\left({ }^n \mathrm{C}_2+2 \cdot{ }^n \mathrm{C}_0\right) \mathrm{x}^2+\ldots \ldots$ Hence, $A_0=1 ; A_1={ }^n C_1 ; A_2={ }^n C_2+2$ which are in A.P.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: In the expansion of $\left(2^{\frac{1}{5}}+7^{\frac{1}{7}}\right)^{105}$, which of the following holds good?
options: Exactly one middle term is irrational.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} T_{r+1} & ={ }^{105} C_r\left(2^{\frac{1}{5}}\right)^{105-r} \cdot 7^{\frac{r}{7}} \\ & ={ }^{105} C_r 2^{\frac{105-r}{5}} \cdot 7^{\frac{r}{7}}\end{aligned}$
Clearly $r$ should be a multiple of 7 and 5 ,
$r=0,35,70,105$
$\Rightarrow \quad$ Number of rational terms $=4$
Number of irrational terms $=102$
Middle term $=T_{53}$ and $T_{54}$ are both irrational.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: In the binomial expansion of $\left(\sqrt{y}+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt[4]{y}}\right)^n$ the first three coefficients form an arithmetic progression. Then:
options: number of terms in the expansion where the power of $y$ is natural is 2
number of terms in the expansion where the power of $y$ is natural is 3
questions.solution:
$\left(\sqrt{y}+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt[4]{y}}\right)^n$
First 3 coefficient are
${ }^n C_0, \frac{{ }^n C_1}{2}, \frac{{ }^n C_2}{2^2}$; Hence $\quad 1+\frac{n(n-1)}{8}=n$
$8+n^2-n=8 n \quad \Rightarrow \quad n^2-9 n+8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n=8$ or $1 \quad(n=1$ is rejected $)$
$n=1$ is rejected $\quad \therefore \quad n=8$
$\therefore \quad$ The given expansion is $\left[y^{\frac{1}{2}}+\frac{1}{2} y^{-\frac{1}{4}}\right]^8$
Where, $T_{r+1}=\frac{{ }^8 C_r}{2^r} \cdot y^{\frac{n-r}{2}} \cdot y^{-\frac{r}{4}}=\frac{{ }^8 C_r}{2^r} y^{\frac{2 n-3 r}{4}}=\frac{{ }^8 C_r}{2^r} \cdot y^{\frac{16-3 r}{4}} \quad$ (using $\left.n=8\right)$
The terms where power of $y$ is natural are
${ }^n C_0 \cdot y^4 \rightarrow$ First term where $r=0$
$\frac{{ }^8 C_4}{2^4} \cdot y^1 \rightarrow$ Fifth terms where $r=4$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Which of the following must hold good for the expansion of the binomial $\left(x^4+\frac{1}{x^3}\right)^{15} ?$
options: There exist a term which is independent of $x$.
$8^{\text {th }}$ and $9^{\text {th }}$ terms of the expansion have the greatest binomial coefficient
If $x=\sqrt{2}$, then number of rational terms in the expansion is 5
questions.solution:
$\because \mathrm{T}_{r+1}{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_r\left(\mathrm{x}^4\right)^{(15-r)}\left(\mathrm{X}^{-3}\right)^r={ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{x}^{80-7 r}$
(A) for the term independent of $x, 60-7 r=0 \Rightarrow r$ is not an integer
$\therefore$ there is no term independent of x .
(B) $\because n=15$ is odd
$\therefore{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}$ will be maximum if $\mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{n}-1}{2}$ or $\mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{n}+1}{2}$
i.e. $r=7$ or $r=8$
$\therefore$ binomial coefficient of $8^{\text {th }}$ and 9 th terms will be greatest
(C) for the cofficient of $x^{32} ; 60-7 r=32 \Rightarrow r=4$
$\therefore$ cofficient of $\mathrm{X}^{32}$ is $={ }^{15} \mathrm{C}$
for the coeff. of $x^{-17} ; 60-7 r=-17 ; r=11$
$\therefore$ coeff. of $\mathrm{x}^{-17}$ is $={ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{11}={ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_4$
$\therefore$ (C) is correct
(D) If $x=\sqrt{2}, T_{r+1}={ }^{15} C_r 2^{\frac{60-7 r}{2}}$
$\therefore$ for rational terms $r=0,2,4,6, \ldots \ldots .14$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Consider $(1+x)^{2 n}+\left(1+2 x+x^2\right)^n=\sum_{r=0}^{2 n} a_r x^r, n \in N$. If $\sum_{r=0}^{2 n} a_r=f(n)$ then:
options: largest value of $p$ for which $f(5)$ is divisible by $2^p$ is 11 .
largest value of $p$ for which $f(5)$ is divisible by $2^p$ is 9 .
questions.solution:
$(1+x)^{2 n}+\left(1+2 x+x^2\right)^n=\sum_{r=0}^{2 n} a_r x^r$
$2(1+x)^{2 n}=\sum_{r=0}^{2 n} a_r x^r$
So,$f(n)=\sum_{r=0}^{2 n} a_r=2^{2 n+1}$  ...(1)
So,$\begin{array}{r}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{f(n)}=\frac{1}{2^3}+\frac{1}{2^5}+\frac{1}{2^7}+\ldots . . \\ =\frac{1 / 2^3}{1-\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{1 / 8}{3 / 4}=\frac{1}{6}\end{array}$
Largest value of $p$ for which $f(5)$ is divisible by $2^p$
$f(5)=2^{11}$
So, $p=11$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If $(4+\sqrt{15})^{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{f}$, where n is an odd natural number, l is an integer and $0 \lt \mathrm{f} \lt 1$, then
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{f}=(4+\sqrt{15})^n$
Let $\mathrm{g}=(4-\sqrt{15})^{\mathrm{n}}$, then $0 \lt \mathrm{g} \lt 1$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{f}={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_0 4^n+{ }^n \mathrm{C}_1 4^{\mathrm{n}-1} \sqrt{15}+{ }^n \mathrm{C}_2 4^{\mathrm{n}-2} 15+{ }^n C_3 4^{\mathrm{n}-3}(\sqrt{15})^3+\ldots . \\ & \mathrm{g}={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_0 4^n-{ }^n C_1 4^{\mathrm{n}-1} \sqrt{15}+{ }^n C_2 4^{\mathrm{n}-2} 15-{ }^n C_3 4^{n-3}(\sqrt{15})^3+\ldots . .\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{f}+\mathrm{g}=2\left({ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_0 4^{\mathrm{n}}+{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_2 4^{\mathrm{n}-2} 15+\ldots ..\right)=$ even integer
$\begin{aligned} & \because 0 \lt \mathrm{f}+\mathrm{g} \lt 2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}+\mathrm{g}=1\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow 1-\mathrm{f}=\mathrm{g}$ thus I is an odd integer $1-\mathrm{f}=\mathrm{g}=(4-\sqrt{15})^{\mathrm{n}}$ $(I+f)(1-f)=(I+f) g=1$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Let $\left(1+x^2\right)^2(1+x)^n=A_0+A_1 x+A_2 x^2+\ldots \ldots$. If $A_0, A_1, A_2$ are in A.P. then the value of $n$ is :
questions.solution:
$\begin{gathered}
\left(1+x^2\right)^2(1+x)^n=\left(1+2 x^2+x^4\right)\left({ }^n C_0+{ }^n C_1 x+{ }^n C_2 x^2+\ldots \ldots\right) \\
={ }^n C_0+{ }^n C_1 x+\left({ }^n C_2+2 .{ }^n C_0\right) x^2+\ldots \ldots
\end{gathered}$
Hence $A_0=1 ; A_1={ }^n C_1 ; A_2={ }^n C_2+2$ which are in A.P.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If $(9+\sqrt{80})^{\mathrm{n}}=1+\mathrm{f}$ where $\mathrm{I}, \mathrm{n}$ are integers and $0 \lt \mathrm{f} \lt 1$, then
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& (9+4 \sqrt{5})^n=3+\mathrm{f} \\
& \text { let }(9-4 \sqrt{5})^n=\mathrm{f}^{\prime} \\
& \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{f}+\mathrm{f}^{\prime}=(9+4 \sqrt{5})^n+(9-4 \sqrt{5})^n=\text { even integer } \\
& 0 \lt \mathrm{f}+\mathrm{f}^{\prime} \lt 2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}+\mathrm{f}^{\prime}=1
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}$ is an odd integer $\Rightarrow(A)$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
